request.question: Were both Elizabeth Chadwick and Connie May Fowler authors?

title: Nancy Fairbanks
Elizabeth Chadwick
Beale family
Alan Chadwick
Connie May Fowler
Stephen F. Chadwick
Theophilus Hastings, 7th Earl of Huntingdon
Before Women Had Wings
Fowler's position
Owen Chadwick
sentences: Nancy Fairbanks Herndon is a US lecturer in English and the author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick and mysteries under the name Nancy Herndon and Nancy Fairbanks.
Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions.
She is a member of Regia Anglorum, a Medieval reenactment organisation.
The Beales, together with the Fowlers, are a fictional family in the BBC soap opera "EastEnders".
They were the main family for storylines in early "EastEnders" and remained as such for many years.
Having appeared continuously from episode one, they are the show's longest serving family.
In 2004 Peter Beale and Lucy Beale were recast to give them a more mature look.
In 2007, there were few members of the Beale family left and no Fowlers, so the family were revamped.
Peter was recast again.
Ian Beale started a relationship with Jane Beale and Steven Beale also returned.
By 2010, the number of Beales had fallen again.
Lucy was recast and in 2013, Peter and Bobby were recast and Cindy Williams Jnr, the half-sister of Peter and Lucy, was reintroduced.
In addition to this at the start of 2014 Bex Fowler returned to the serial with her mother Sonia Fowler, with Martin Fowler following 11 months later in December.
In 2014 Lucy is found dead, causing her family and friends to struggle to come to terms with her death and the secrets she left behind, putting many people in the frame for her murder.
It was revealed on 19 February 2015 that she was killed by her own little brother, Bobby.
It was also revealed that Kathy Beale, who was presumed dead in 2006, was alive after having faked her death with her new husband in South Africa.
In December 2016 Michelle Fowler returned to Albert Square after originally leaving in 1995, although now played by Jenna Russell.
Members of the Beale family by blood currently in "EastEnders" include Ian, Michelle, Martin, Bex and Louie.
Additionally, several others may be considered Beales through marriage; Jane, Kathy, Sonia and Stacey Fowler are among these.
Also, Whitney Dean was adopted by Bianca Butcher, who is a member of the Beale family.
Lily Fowler and Arthur Fowler have both taken Martin's surname and have him as their stepfather.

Alan Chadwick (July 27, 1909 – May 25, 1980), English master gardener, was a leading innovator of organic farming techniques and influential educator in the field of biodynamic/French intensive gardening.
He was a student of Rudolf Steiner and is often cited as inspirational to the development of the "California Cuisine" movement.
The Chadwick restaurant in Beverly Hills was named after him.
His grave is marked by a stupa at the Green Gulch Farm Zen Center in California.

Chadwick is the subject of a 2013 retrospective by a former University of California, Santa Cruz professor, Paul Lee, called "There Is a Garden in the Mind: A Memoir of Alan Chadwick and the Organic Movement in California".
There is a memorial page at http://www.alan-chadwick.org/ that includes the 1971 16-mm film "Garden".
Connie May Fowler (born January 3, 1960 to parents of multi-cultural backgrounds) is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet.
Her semi-autobiographical novel, "Before Women had Wings", received the 1996 Southern Book Critics Circle Award and the Francis Buck Award (League of American Pen Women).
She adapted the novel for Oprah Winfrey and the subsequent Emmy-winning film starred Winfrey, Ellen Barkin, Julia Stiles, and Tina Majorino.
"Remembering Blue" received the Chautauqua South Literary Award.
Three of her novels were Dublin International Literary Award nominees.
Her other novels include "Sugar Cage" and "River of Hidden Dreams".
"The Problem with Murmur Lee" was Redbook’s premier book club selection.
Her memoir, "When Katie Wakes", explores her family’s generational cycle of domestic violence.
"How Clarissa Burden Learned to Fly", a novel oft compared to Virginia Woolf's "Mrs. Dalloway" in term of its structure, was published in 2010.
Her latest book, a memoir titled "A Million Fragile bones," will be published April 20, 2017 by Twisted Road Publications.
It explores her life on an isolated barrier island and the horrific impact and aftermath of the Deepwater Horizon BP oil spill.
Her books have been translated into eighteen languages (http://www.conniemayfowler.com/about.html).
Stephen Fowler Chadwick (December 25, 1825 – January 15, 1895) was an American Democratic politician who served as the fifth Governor of Oregon from 1877 to 1878.
Governor Chadwick was the first person to obtain the governorship by way of the state's Line of Succession.
Theophilus Hastings, 7th Earl of Huntingdon PC (10 December 1650 – 30 May 1701) was an English politician.
He was the son of Ferdinando Hastings, 6th Earl of Huntingdon, born in the 27th year of his parents' marriage, and became Earl of Huntingdon on 13 February 1656 on his father's death.
He married, firstly, Elizabeth Lewis, daughter of Sir John Lewis, 1st Baronet, on 19 February 1671/2.
She died 24th December 1688.
He married, secondly, Mary Frances Fowler, daughter of Francis Leveson Fowler, on 8 May 1690.
With his first wife he had two sons and six daughters, including Thomas (1674–1675), George and Elizabeth.
With his second wife he had two more sons and five more daughters, including Theophilus, Catherine, Maria and Margaret.
Before Women Had Wings is a 1997 television film, based on the story by Connie May Fowler about a mother whose abusive husband commits suicide.
She then starts to violently abuse her two daughters.
It is only then that a kindly black woman becomes good friends with her youngest daughter and helps the daughter escape her tortured life.
The film stars Ellen Barkin, Oprah Winfrey, Tina Majorino, and Julia Stiles.
Barkin won an Emmy for her role.
In medicine, Fowler's position is a standard patient position.
It is an intervention used to promote oxygenation via maximum chest expansion and is implemented during events of respiratory distress.
Fowler's position facilitates the relaxing of tension of the abdominal muscles, allowing for improved breathing.
In immobile patients and infants, the Fowler's position alleviates compression of the chest that occurs due to gravity.
Fowler's position increases comfort during eating and other activities, is used in postpartum women to improve uterine drainage, and in infants when signs of respiratory distress present.
The patient is placed in a semi-upright sitting position (45-60 degrees) and may have knees either bent or straight.
Fowler's position is also used when oral or nasal gastric feeding tubes have been implemented as it minimizes the risk of aspiration.
Peristalsis and swallowing are aided by the effect of gravitational pull.
William Owen Chadwick ( ; 20 May 1916 – 17 July 2015) was a British Anglican priest, academic, writer and prominent historian of Christianity.
As a leading academic, Chadwick became Dixie Professor of Ecclesiastical History from 1958 to 1968 and Regius Professor of History from 1968 to 1983.
From 1956 to 1983, Chadwick was elected and severed as the Master of Selwyn College, Cambridge.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

hop1_docs: Elizabeth Chadwick | Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions.  She is a member of Regia Anglorum, a Medieval reenactment organisation.

Stephen F. Chadwick | Stephen Fowler Chadwick (December 25, 1825 – January 15, 1895) was an American Democratic politician who served as the fifth Governor of Oregon from 1877 to 1878.  Governor Chadwick was the first person to obtain the governorship by way of the state's Line of Succession.
Lilian Fowler | Elizabeth Lilian Maud Fowler MBE, JP (7 June 1886 – 11 May 1954) was an Australian politician, and Australia's first female mayor.

Nancy Fairbanks | Nancy Fairbanks Herndon is a US lecturer in English and the author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick and mysteries under the name Nancy Herndon and Nancy Fairbanks.

Connie May Fowler | Connie May Fowler (born January 3, 1960 to parents of multi-cultural backgrounds) is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet.  Her semi-autobiographical novel, "Before Women had Wings", received the 1996 Southern Book Critics Circle Award and the Francis Buck Award (League of American Pen Women).  She adapted the novel for Oprah Winfrey and the subsequent Emmy-winning film starred Winfrey, Ellen Barkin, Julia Stiles, and Tina Majorino.  "Remembering Blue" received the Chautauqua South Literary Award.  Three of her novels were Dublin International Literary Award nominees.  Her other novels include "Sugar Cage" and "River of Hidden Dreams".  "The Problem with Murmur Lee" was Redbook’s premier book club selection.  Her memoir, "When Katie Wakes", explores her family’s generational cycle of domestic violence.  "How Clarissa Burden Learned to Fly", a novel oft compared to Virginia Woolf's "Mrs. Dalloway" in term of its structure, was published in 2010.  Her latest book, a memoir titled "A Million Fragile bones," will be published April 20, 2017 by Twisted Road Publications.  It explores her life on an isolated barrier island and the horrific impact and aftermath of the Deepwater Horizon BP oil spill.  Her books have been translated into eighteen languages (http://www.conniemayfowler.com/about.html).

Connie Needham | Connie Needham (born December 5, 1959) is an American actress and dance instructor.  As Connie Newton, she portrayed Elizabeth Bradford on the television series "Eight is Enough" from 1977 to 1981.
Conny Van Dyke | Conny Van Dyke (sometimes credited as Connie Van Dyke) is a singer and actress, born September 28, 1945 in Nassawadox, Virginia to Benjamin and Charlotte Elizabeth Van Dyke.
response.summary_1:
- Fact: [1] — "Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions." (quote)
- Fact: [4] — Nancy Fairbanks Herndon is "the author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick." (quote)
- Fact: [5] — "is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet." (Connie May Fowler) (quote)

Bridge entities and ambiguity:
- Elizabeth Chadwick — AMBIGUOUS: appears as a standalone author page ([1]) and as a pseudonym for Nancy Fairbanks Herndon ([4]); identity linkage unclear.
- Connie May Fowler — UNAMBIGUOUS author per passage [5].

Next-hop retrieval targets (prioritized):
1) Fetch the full "Elizabeth Chadwick" page (if distinct) — check lead/intro, "Also known as"/pseudonyms, birth name, and bibliography/works to confirm identity and whether it's a real name or pen name.
2) Fetch the "Nancy Fairbanks" / "Nancy Herndon" page (passage 4) — check lead/intro and pseudonyms section for explicit statement that she uses "Elizabeth Chadwick" and whether that is the same person as the Elizabeth Chadwick page.
3) If ambiguous remains, fetch an "Elizabeth Chadwick (disambiguation)" page or library authority records (e.g., VIAF/LCNAF) — extract name variants, dates, and linked works to reconcile the two entries.

No final yes/no answer provided here because Elizabeth Chadwick's identity is ambiguous across the supplied passages.
response.hop2_query: Elizabeth Chadwick page confirm pseudonym Nancy Fairbanks Herndon
hop2_docs: Nancy Fairbanks | Nancy Fairbanks Herndon is a US lecturer in English and the author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick and mysteries under the name Nancy Herndon and Nancy Fairbanks.
Elizabeth Chadwick | Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions.  She is a member of Regia Anglorum, a Medieval reenactment organisation.

Adrienne McNeil Herndon | Adrienne Elizabeth McNeil Herndon was an actress, professor, and activist in Atlanta, Georgia.  While admittedly an African American to friends and colleagues, she performed with the stage name Anne Du Bignon and claimed French-Creole heritage.  She was one of the first African American faculty at the University of Atlanta, where she met W. E. B. Du Bois and subsequently worked with him.  Herndon and Alonzo Herndon were an influential couple.

Bound in Morocco | Bound in Morocco is a 1918 American silent action comedy romance film starring Douglas Fairbanks.  Fairbanks produced and wrote the film's story and screenplay (under the pseudonym Elton Thomas), and Allan Dwan directed.  The film was produced by Douglas Fairbanks Pictures Corporation and distributed by Famous Players-Lasky/Artcraft Pictures.

Laura Lee Hope | Laura Lee Hope is a pseudonym used by the Stratemeyer Syndicate for the Bobbsey Twins and several other series of children's novels.  Actual writers taking up the pen of Laura Lee Hope include Edward Stratemeyer, Howard and Lilian Garis, Elizabeth Ward, Harriet (Stratemeyer) Adams, Andrew E. Svenson, June M. Dunn, Grace Grote and Nancy Axelrad.

Alan Chadwick | Alan Chadwick (July 27, 1909 – May 25, 1980), English master gardener, was a leading innovator of organic farming techniques and influential educator in the field of biodynamic/French intensive gardening.  He was a student of Rudolf Steiner and is often cited as inspirational to the development of the "California Cuisine" movement.  The Chadwick restaurant in Beverly Hills was named after him.  His grave is marked by a stupa at the Green Gulch Farm Zen Center in California.  Chadwick is the subject of a 2013 retrospective by a former University of California, Santa Cruz professor, Paul Lee, called "There Is a Garden in the Mind: A Memoir of Alan Chadwick and the Organic Movement in California".  There is a memorial page at http://www.alan-chadwick.org/ that includes the 1971 16-mm film "Garden".
Nancy Elizabeth | Nancy Elizabeth Cunliffe (born 1983) is a folk singer-songwriter and multi-instrumentalist, who releases music with The Leaf Label.  She is an active member of Sōka Gakkai.
response.summary_2: Yes — Elizabeth Chadwick and Connie May Fowler are both authors. Elizabeth Chadwick is described in the passages as an author of historical fictions and is also recorded as a pseudonym used by Nancy Fairbanks Herndon for historical romances.
